evidence_quotes.quote: Their digital product, largely taking the form of video games, should not be susceptible to forceful removal by their desire to resell a newer and more hostile version of the same product.

The products most susceptible to this forcefully planned obsolescence are also often the ones most likely to contain extra sub-purchases, leading to an underlying collection of extra digital goods which ostensibly would be perceived as belonging to the customer, which are removed from them without recourse at the whims of the corporate machinery.
End User License Agreements which contain more text than any layperson would be willing to read

paragraphs.text: Hello I wish to express my opinion as an EU citizen who lives largely in the digital space. For too long now, have corporate interests been allowed to largely control the shape of the digital landscape of the internet. Corporate interests which only seek one thing: Profit, and what's more profitable than to force your customers out of a choice. This is why I believe the DFA ought to be expanded to cover the protection of sold digital goods from willful remote destruction at the behest of international corporations. Their digital product, largely taking the form of video games, should not be susceptible to forceful removal by their desire to resell a newer and more hostile version of the same product. The products most susceptible to this forcefully planned obsolence are also often the ones most likely to contain extra sub-purchases, leading to an underlying collection of extra digital goods which ostensibly would be perceived as belonging to the customer, which are removed from them without recourse at the whims of the corporate machinery. As it stands these corporate interests hide away clauses to shield themselves in End User License Agreements which contain more text than any layperson would be willing to read, this is a known and tangible problem and in itself should see more legislation than it does. Similarly, an influx of personal moral judgement and censorship has besieged the communication of the digital world, in the form of cartel-like payment processor demands to withhold fully lawful products from the market based solely on drummed up outrage masquerading religious propaganda. Payment processors in this day and age are too vitally embedded in the infrastructure of modern life to be allowed to weigh in on private citizens ability to make lawful purchases. It may begin in places many are unwilling to speak about openly in a political setting, but if we give an inch, they will take a mile. First they will come for the scandalous, then the questionable, and soon we will be controlled illegally from seeing any opinion that does not tow the company policy. This is a vital issue of corporate censorship on private citizens, and if it is not already a breach of several laws, it is highly immoral and legislation needs to be put in place to ensure freedom of choice and information. Lest we face a future where our every action is dictated to us by the machinery of corporate greed. No citizen of a free democratic nation should have to wake up in fear of finding that they are prohibited by international corporations from partaking in entertainment which in all legislation is considered lawful, nor should they have to fear that their livelihood has been made ostensibly illegal due to that same corporations decisions on what morality it is choosing to enforce unto others, often highly hypocritically as these corporations themselves subscribe to no morality in their actions.
